questions.question: What is the supreme law of India?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India is the supreme law of the land. All other laws must be consistent with it.

questions.question: How many fundamental rights are guaranteed by the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution guarantees 6 Fundamental Rights under Part III (Articles 12-35).

questions.question: How many languages are listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Eighth Schedule lists 22 official languages of India.

questions.question: Right to Education is guaranteed under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 21A (added by 86th Amendment, 2002) provides free and compulsory education to children aged 6-14 years.

questions.question: What is the national animal of India?
questions.explanation: Bengal Tiger (Panthera tigris tigris) is the national animal of India.

questions.question: Which writ is issued by a court to compel a public authority to perform its legal duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus (meaning 'We command') is issued to a public authority, lower court, or corporation to perform a public or statutory duty.

questions.question: Which writ is used to produce a detained person before a court?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus (meaning 'You may have the body') is issued to free a person from illegal detention.

questions.question: How many schedules does the Indian Constitution have?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution originally had 8 schedules; it now has 12 schedules after various amendments.

questions.question: Which schedule of the Constitution deals with anti-defection law?
questions.explanation: The Tenth Schedule (added by the 52nd Amendment, 1985) contains provisions for disqualification on grounds of defection.

questions.question: Article 21 of the Indian Constitution guarantees:
options: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
questions.explanation: Article 21 states: 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.'

questions.question: The Right to Property was removed from the list of Fundamental Rights by which amendment?
questions.explanation: The 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) removed the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights and made it a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: Which constitutional body is known as the 'watchdog of public finance'?
questions.explanation: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), under Article 148, audits all government accounts and is called the watchdog of public finance.

questions.question: Which article guarantees freedom of speech and expression in India?
questions.explanation: Article 19(1)(a) guarantees all citizens the right to freedom of speech and expression.

questions.question: The Seventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution deals with:
options: Distribution of powers between Centre and States
questions.explanation: The Seventh Schedule contains three lists — Union List, State List, and Concurrent List — which distribute legislative powers between the Centre and States.

questions.question: Which amendment reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years?
questions.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment Act (1988) reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years.

questions.question: The Constitution of India originally had:
questions.explanation: The original Constitution (1950) had 395 articles, 22 parts, and 8 schedules. After amendments, it now has 448 articles, 25 parts, and 12 schedules. It is the world's lengthiest written constitution.

questions.question: India borrowed the concept of 'Fundamental Rights' from:
questions.explanation: India borrowed the concept of Fundamental Rights from the US Constitution (First 10 Amendments — Bill of Rights). The idea of judicial review was also borrowed from the USA.

questions.question: Fundamental Rights are enshrined in:
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights are in Part III (Articles 12–35) of the Indian Constitution. They are justiciable (enforceable by courts). Originally 7, now 6 (Right to Property removed by 44th Amendment 1978, made a legal right under Article 300A).

questions.question: The six Fundamental Rights are:
options: Right to Equality, Right to Freedom, Right against Exploitation, Right to Freedom of Religion, Cultural & Educational Rights, Right to Constitutional Remedies
Right to Property, Right to Education, Right to Equality, Right to Vote, Right to Work, Right to Freedom
Right to Life, Right to Liberty, Right to Property, Right to Religion, Right to Speech, Right to Assembly
Right to Equality, Right to Work, Right to Education, Right to Food, Right to Health, Right to Housing
questions.explanation: The 6 Fundamental Rights: 1) Right to Equality (Art 14-18), 2) Right to Freedom (Art 19-22), 3) Right against Exploitation (Art 23-24), 4) Right to Freedom of Religion (Art 25-28), 5) Cultural and Educational Rights (Art 29-30), 6) Right to Constitutional Remedies (Art 32).

questions.question: Article 14 of the Indian Constitution guarantees:
options: Right to Equality before law and Equal protection of laws
Right to Life and Personal Liberty
questions.explanation: Article 14: 'The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.' It is available to citizens AND non-citizens.

questions.question: Article 19 guarantees freedom to all CITIZENS. Which of these is NOT in Article 19?
options: Freedom to reside and settle anywhere in India
Right to life and personal liberty
questions.explanation: Article 19 gives citizens 6 freedoms: speech/expression, peaceful assembly (without arms), forming associations/unions, free movement, residence/settlement, and practising any profession/occupation. Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) is separate.

questions.question: Article 21 guarantees:
options: Protection of life and personal liberty — 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law'
questions.explanation: Article 21 is one of the most fundamental rights. Through judicial interpretation, it now covers: right to privacy, right to livelihood, right to health, right to clean environment, right to speedy trial, right to dignity, etc. Article 21A guarantees free education for children 6-14 years.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) was added by the:
questions.explanation: The 86th Constitutional Amendment Act (2002) inserted Article 21A: 'The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years.' The Right to Education Act 2009 gives effect to this.

questions.question: 'Habeas Corpus' literally means:
options: You may have the body — a writ ordering the production of a detained person before court to determine if detention is lawful
A question of right — challenging a person's claim to public office
It is prohibited — to stop lower court/tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus ('you may have the body') is a writ ordering the production of a detained person before a court to determine if the detention is lawful. It protects against arbitrary arrest and detention.

questions.question: The writ 'Mandamus' is issued to:
options: Lower courts to stop exceeding jurisdiction
A public official or body to perform a legal duty they have neglected or refused to perform
A person to justify why they hold a public office
Produce a detained person before court
questions.explanation: Mandamus ('we command') is a writ directing a public official, lower court, government body, or corporation to perform a mandatory public duty that it has failed or refused to perform. Cannot be issued against a private person.

questions.question: Equal pay for equal work for both men and women is under:
questions.explanation: Article 39(d) DPSP: 'equal pay for equal work for both men and women.' Though not a Fundamental Right, the Supreme Court has interpreted Article 39(d) in conjunction with Article 14 and 16 to enforce equal pay.

questions.question: Question Hour in Parliament is held:
options: During the first hour of every sitting day (11 AM-12 noon)
questions.explanation: Question Hour is the first hour (11 AM-12 noon) of each Parliamentary sitting. Members can ask oral questions (starred — answered orally, supplementaries can follow) and written questions (unstarred — written answers). Zero Hour immediately follows.

questions.question: The President of India can declare National Emergency under:
options: Article 352 — if national security is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion
questions.explanation: Article 352 allows the President to proclaim National Emergency if the security of India is threatened by war, external aggression, or 'armed rebellion' (changed from 'internal disturbance' by 44th Amendment). Cabinet must advise in writing. Requires 2/3 special majority in Parliament.

questions.question: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) restricted the use of National Emergency by:
options: Requiring a written recommendation by the Cabinet + 2/3 majority of total membership in each House (changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion')
questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment (1978, post-Emergency) changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion' (harder standard), required Cabinet's written recommendation, required approval by special majority (2/3 of total membership), and gave Lok Sabha power to revoke Emergency by simple majority.

questions.question: India is described as:
options: A Union of States (quasi-federal — strong centre, federal features)
questions.explanation: Article 1 describes India as 'a Union of States'. India has federal features (two levels of government, written constitution, division of powers, independent judiciary) but also strong unitary features (single citizenship, integrated judiciary, emergency powers). K.C. Wheare called it 'quasi-federal'.

questions.question: The concept of Fundamental Rights in Indian Constitution is borrowed from:
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights (Part III) were largely inspired by the Bill of Rights in the US Constitution (First 10 Amendments). The judicial review mechanism also came from the USA.

questions.question: The concept of 'Concurrent List' in Indian Constitution is borrowed from:
questions.explanation: The three-list system (Union, State, Concurrent) and specifically the Concurrent List was borrowed from the Australian Constitution.

questions.question: India's Constitution is the world's longest written constitution with originally how many articles?
questions.explanation: Originally the Constitution had 395 articles in 22 parts and 8 schedules. Currently it has 448 articles (as amended) in 25 parts and 12 schedules (as of 2023).

questions.question: The original Constitution had how many Schedules?
questions.explanation: Original Constitution (1950) had 8 Schedules. Currently it has 12 Schedules (4 new schedules added by various amendments).

questions.question: Which schedule lists the official languages of India?
questions.explanation: 8th Schedule originally listed 14 official languages. Currently it has 22 languages. Tamil was one of the original 14 languages.

questions.question: The 9th Schedule was added by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.explanation: 9th Schedule was added by the 1st Constitutional Amendment (1951) to protect certain land reform laws from judicial review. Laws in 9th Schedule are immune from fundamental rights challenge.

questions.question: Right to Education (RTE) is a Fundamental Right under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 21A (inserted by 86th Amendment, 2002) makes free and compulsory education for children 6-14 years a Fundamental Right. The RTE Act 2009 gives effect to this right.

questions.question: Article 19 guarantees freedom of:
options: Speech, assembly, movement, profession, and more (6 freedoms)
questions.explanation: Article 19 guarantees 6 freedoms: (a) Speech & Expression, (b) Peaceful Assembly, (c) Form Associations, (d) Move freely, (e) Reside anywhere, (g) Practice any profession/trade.

questions.question: The Right to Property was removed as a Fundamental Right by:
questions.explanation: 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) removed Right to Property from Fundamental Rights (Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31). It became a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: Freedom of religion is guaranteed by Articles:
questions.explanation: Articles 25-28 deal with Freedom of Religion: Art. 25 (right to profess, practice, propagate), Art. 26 (manage religious affairs), Art. 27 (no tax for religion), Art. 28 (no religious instruction in state schools).

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right is available to both citizens and non-citizens?
questions.explanation: Article 14 (Equality before law) applies to 'any person' — both citizens and non-citizens. Similarly, Articles 20, 21, 21A, 22, 25-28 apply to all persons, not just citizens.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right cannot be suspended even during Emergency?
questions.explanation: Under Article 359, during Emergency, rights under Article 19 can be suspended, but Articles 20 (protection against conviction for offenses) and 21 (right to life) CANNOT be suspended even during Emergency.

questions.question: The 'Golden Triangle' of Fundamental Rights refers to:
questions.explanation: Articles 14 (equality), 19 (freedoms), and 21 (life and personal liberty) are called the 'Golden Triangle' as they are most frequently invoked and mutually reinforce each other.

questions.question: The Minerva Mills case (1980) established that:
options: Parliament cannot destroy Fundamental Rights by amending DPSPs
Preamble is not part of Constitution
questions.explanation: Minerva Mills case (1980): SC held that Parliament cannot destroy the balance between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs — the 42nd Amendment's attempt to give DPSPs absolute primacy was unconstitutional.

questions.question: Which Parliamentary procedure is associated with 'starred questions'?
questions.explanation: Starred questions (marked with *) require oral answers and are followed by supplementary questions. Unstarred questions require written answers and no supplementary questions are allowed.

questions.question: The President's term of office is:
questions.explanation: The President holds office for 5 years (Article 56). Can be re-elected any number of times. Can resign by writing to Vice President.

questions.question: Cut motions on Demands for Grants are of how many types?
questions.explanation: Three types of Cut Motions: (1) Disapproval of Policy Cut (reduce to ₹1), (2) Economy Cut (reduce by specific amount), (3) Token Cut (reduce by ₹100 to ventilate grievance).

questions.question: The concept of 'due process of law' in India was elaborated in:
options: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
questions.explanation: Maneka Gandhi case (1978): SC interpreted Article 21 broadly — procedure for depriving life/liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable (not mere 'procedure established by law' as literally stated).

questions.question: Which case established that Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017): 9-judge bench unanimously held that Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21.

questions.question: Article 13 of the Constitution deals with:
options: Laws inconsistent with or in derogation of Fundamental Rights are void
questions.explanation: Article 13: Any law that takes away or abridges Fundamental Rights is void to the extent of inconsistency. It also prohibits the State from making such laws — foundation of judicial review.

questions.question: The 'Right to be forgotten' relates to:
questions.explanation: 'Right to be forgotten' (right to erasure of personal data from internet) flows from Right to Privacy under Article 21, as elaborated in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).

questions.question: 'Stare Decisis' means:
questions.explanation: Stare decisis (Latin: 'stand by what has been decided') is the doctrine of precedent — courts follow earlier decisions (especially of higher courts) in similar cases.

questions.question: 'Letter Jurisdiction' of High Courts is under:
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts power to issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Certiorari, Prohibition, Quo Warranto) for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and other legal rights.

questions.question: Lok Adalats (People's Courts) are established under:
questions.explanation: Lok Adalats are established under the Legal Services Authorities Act 1987 to provide alternative dispute resolution. Awards made by Lok Adalat are deemed decrees and final, not appealable.

questions.question: Fifth Schedule of the Constitution deals with:
options: Administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes
questions.explanation: 5th Schedule: Administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in states (most states). 6th Schedule: Administration of Tribal Areas in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram.

questions.question: The Planning Commission was replaced by:
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) replaced the Planning Commission on 1 January 2015. Unlike Planning Commission, NITI Aayog is an advisory body without power to allocate funds.

questions.question: Which writ is known as 'Bulwark of personal liberty'?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus (Latin: 'to have the body') is called the great writ/bulwark of personal liberty. It directs the detaining authority to produce the detained person before court and justify detention.

questions.question: Writ of Prohibition is issued to:
options: Prohibit a lower court from exceeding jurisdiction
questions.explanation: Writ of Prohibition is issued to prevent a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction or usurping jurisdiction it does not have. It is issued before judgment.

questions.question: Can High Courts issue all 5 writs?
options: Yes, under Article 226 (and for FRs under Article 226)
No, only SC can issue all 5
HC can issue only Habeas Corpus
HC can issue writs only for FRs
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts power to issue all 5 writs — not just for enforcement of Fundamental Rights but also for 'any other purpose'. SC under Article 32 issues writs only for FR enforcement.

questions.question: National Green Tribunal (NGT) was established in:
questions.explanation: National Green Tribunal Act 2010 established the NGT for effective and speedy disposal of cases relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests.

questions.question: Which committee drafted the Fundamental Rights provisions?
options: Fundamental Rights Sub-Committee under J.B. Kripalani
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Rights Sub-Committee was chaired by J.B. Kripalani. Sardar Patel chaired the Advisory Committee on Fundamental Rights and Minorities.

questions.question: Article 21 of the Constitution guarantees:
options: Protection of life and personal liberty
questions.explanation: Article 21 states no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law. Post-Maneka Gandhi (1978), this procedure must be fair and reasonable.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) applies to children in the age group:
questions.explanation: Article 21A (inserted by 86th Amendment, 2002) provides free and compulsory education to children in the age of 6-14 years. The RTE Act 2009 implemented this right.

questions.question: Freedom of press in India is derived from which Article?
questions.explanation: Freedom of the press is implicitly included in Article 19(1)(a) — Freedom of speech and expression. The Supreme Court has held press freedom is a part of this fundamental right.

questions.question: Under which Article can Parliament restrict fundamental rights during a National Emergency?
questions.explanation: Art 358 suspends Art 19 during external emergency automatically. Art 359 allows President to suspend enforcement of other FRs (except Arts 20 & 21) by order during any emergency.

questions.question: 'Double Jeopardy' protection in India is guaranteed under:
questions.explanation: Article 20(2) protects against double jeopardy — no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. This is a fundamental right available to all persons.

questions.question: The 'Question Hour' in Parliament is:
options: The first hour of every parliamentary sitting
The last hour of every sitting
A special session for questions only
questions.explanation: Question Hour is the first hour of a parliamentary sitting (11 AM to 12 Noon in Lok Sabha). Starred questions get oral answers; unstarred questions get written answers.

questions.question: Under which Article can the President seek the Supreme Court's advisory opinion?
questions.explanation: Article 143 allows the President to refer questions of law or fact of public importance to the Supreme Court for its advisory/consultative opinion. The SC may or may not give the opinion.

questions.question: Which Article empowers the High Court to issue writs?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs (including Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto) to any person or authority for enforcement of fundamental rights or any other legal right.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to release a person from illegal detention?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus (Latin: 'you may have the body') is issued by a court to bring a person before it to determine if the detention is lawful. It is the most important writ for personal liberty.

questions.question: Which writ commands a public authority to perform a public or statutory duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus (Latin: 'we command') is issued by a superior court to an inferior court, tribunal, or public authority to compel them to perform a ministerial/statutory duty that they have failed to perform.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to challenge the legal authority of a person to hold a public office?
questions.explanation: Quo Warranto (Latin: 'by what authority') is issued to inquire by what authority a person claims to hold a public office. If the claim is found invalid, the person can be ousted from the office.

questions.question: How many times has the National Emergency (Art 352) been proclaimed in India?
questions.explanation: National Emergency was proclaimed 3 times: (1) 1962 — Chinese aggression; (2) 1971 — Pakistan war; (3) 1975 — Internal disturbance (the controversial Emergency under Indira Gandhi).

questions.question: Which Amendment reversed most of the changes made by the 42nd Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment (1978) by the Janata government reversed many changes of the 42nd Amendment — restored SC/HC powers, changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion', removed Right to Property as FR.

questions.question: The voting age in India was reduced from 21 to 18 years by:
questions.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment (1988) reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years by amending Article 326. India's first Lok Sabha election with 18-year voters was in 1989.

questions.question: The 52nd Amendment (1985) relates to:
questions.explanation: The 52nd Amendment (1985) introduced the Anti-Defection Law by adding the 10th Schedule to the Constitution. It was passed during Rajiv Gandhi's tenure to curb political defections.

questions.question: The 100th Constitutional Amendment (2015) relates to:
questions.explanation: The 100th Amendment (2015) enabled implementation of the Land Boundary Agreement with Bangladesh — territories were exchanged, and the First Schedule was amended to update India's boundary.

questions.question: Which Amendment gave Delhi (NCT) its own legislature and Council of Ministers?
questions.explanation: The 69th Amendment (1991) inserted Arts 239AA and 239AB, giving Delhi (NCT) a Legislature and Council of Ministers. Delhi's legislature can legislate on all matters except police, public order and land.

questions.question: Article 227 empowers the High Court to:
options: Superintend all courts and tribunals within the State
Hear appeals from District Courts only
questions.explanation: Article 227 gives the High Court superintendence over all courts and tribunals within its territorial jurisdiction. This is an administrative power (vs. Art 226's judicial writ power).

questions.question: The 15th Finance Commission's recommendations cover the period:
questions.explanation: The 15th Finance Commission (Chairman: N.K. Singh) submitted its report covering 2021-2026. It recommended 41% devolution of taxes to States (same as 14th FC's 42%, reduced by 1% due to J&K reorganization).

questions.question: NITI Aayog replaced which body in 2015?
questions.explanation: NITI (National Institution for Transforming India) Aayog replaced the Planning Commission on 1 January 2015. Unlike Planning Commission, NITI Aayog is a policy think-tank and does not allocate funds directly to States.

questions.question: In which case did the Supreme Court rule that the Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right?
options: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
questions.explanation: In K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017), a 9-judge bench unanimously held that Right to Privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21. This overruled M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) cases.

questions.question: India follows the principle of:
questions.explanation: India provides for single citizenship — a person can be a citizen of India only, not of any State. This is unlike the USA where citizens have both national and state citizenship.

questions.question: The Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) 2019 provides citizenship to persecuted minorities from:
questions.explanation: The CAA 2019 provides a path to Indian citizenship for Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan who entered India before 31 December 2014.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Colourable Legislation' means:
options: Legislature cannot do indirectly what it cannot do directly
All laws must be in one color/language
Parliament alone can make important laws
questions.explanation: Doctrine of Colourable Legislation: what cannot be done directly cannot be done indirectly. If a legislature lacks power to enact a law, it cannot achieve the same purpose through a differently-colored (worded) law.

questions.question: 'Locus Standi' in PIL means:
options: Anyone can file a petition in public interest, not just the aggrieved person
Only the aggrieved party can file a petition
Judges can initiate cases suo motu
Lawyers have precedence in filing cases
questions.explanation: PIL (Public Interest Litigation) relaxed the traditional rule of locus standi (only aggrieved person can sue). In PIL, any public-spirited citizen or organization can approach the court for public interest matters.

questions.question: The first case of PIL in India was filed by:
options: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar (1979)
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1987)
questions.explanation: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar (1979) is considered the first PIL in India, filed by advocate Kapila Hingorani for undertrial prisoners. Justice P.N. Bhagwati was instrumental in developing PIL jurisprudence.

questions.question: The National Commission for Women was established in:
questions.explanation: The National Commission for Women was established under the National Commission for Women Act, 1990 and became operational from 31 January 1992. It reviews constitutional safeguards for women.

questions.question: Right to Information Act (RTI) came into force in:
questions.explanation: The Right to Information Act was passed in June 2005 and came into force on 12 October 2005. It replaced the Freedom of Information Act, 2002. Citizens can request information from any public authority.

questions.question: The National Green Tribunal (NGT) was established in:
questions.explanation: The National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 established the NGT for effective and speedy disposal of cases relating to environmental protection. It has original, appellate, and settlement jurisdiction in environmental matters.

questions.question: Which writ prevents a lower court from proceeding in a matter outside its jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Prohibition prevents a lower court/tribunal from exercising jurisdiction it does not have or from exceeding its jurisdiction. Certiorari quashes an order already passed; Prohibition prevents the order from being passed.

questions.question: The 'Golden Triangle' of the Indian Constitution comprises:
questions.explanation: The 'Golden Triangle' or 'Trinity' of the Constitution comprises Articles 14 (Equality), 19 (Freedoms), and 21 (Life and Personal Liberty). These are considered the most important Fundamental Rights, often read together.

questions.question: Which country's constitution is the largest in the world?
questions.explanation: India's Constitution is the largest written constitution in the world — originally with 395 articles, 22 parts, 8 schedules. Currently it has 448 articles (25 parts) and 12 schedules after numerous amendments.

questions.question: Which Schedule deals with the administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes?
questions.explanation: The 5th Schedule deals with administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in States other than Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram. The 6th Schedule covers tribal areas of those 4 northeastern States.

questions.question: The office of the District Collector/Collector in India was introduced by:
options: British East India Company (Lord Cornwallis system)
questions.explanation: The office of the Collector was introduced by the British East India Company — primarily by Lord Cornwallis through the Permanent Settlement (1793) system in Bengal. The Collector collects revenue and administers the district.

questions.question: The concept of 'Equality before law' under Article 14 is borrowed from:
options: British Constitution (Rule of Law by Dicey)
questions.explanation: Article 14's first expression — 'Equality before law' — is borrowed from the British concept of Rule of Law formulated by A.V. Dicey. The second expression — 'Equal protection of laws' — is borrowed from the 14th Amendment of the US Constitution.

questions.question: Article 32 (Right to move Supreme Court) is itself a:
questions.explanation: Article 32 — the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights — is itself a Fundamental Right under Part III. It cannot be suspended except during National Emergency (Art 359 order by President).

questions.question: Which case held that Fundamental Rights cannot be amended so as to take away or abridge them?
questions.explanation: In the Golak Nath Case (1967), the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot amend Part III (Fundamental Rights) of the Constitution. This was later overruled in Kesavananda Bharati case.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution provides that the State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or equal protection of the laws?
questions.explanation: Article 14 guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of laws — a core part of Right to Equality.

questions.question: Under which article can the Supreme Court issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Quo Warranto, Certiorari) for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Article 226 gives similar power to High Courts.

questions.question: Which schedule of the Indian Constitution contains the Oaths and Affirmations?
questions.explanation: The Third Schedule contains forms of oaths or affirmations for office-bearers including constitutional functionaries.

questions.question: Under which article can the President refer a question of law to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to refer any question of law or fact of public importance to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion (consultative jurisdiction).

questions.question: Which part of the Indian Constitution deals with Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Part III (Articles 12-35) of the Constitution contains the Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is established under:
options: The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
questions.explanation: NHRC was established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. Its chairman is always a retired Chief Justice of India.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution deals with the Right to Freedom of Religion?
questions.explanation: Articles 25-28 deal with the Right to Freedom of Religion: freedom of conscience, freedom to manage religious affairs, freedom from religious taxes, and religious instruction in educational institutions.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Pith and Substance' helps in resolving:
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Pith and Substance is used to determine the true nature and character of a law when it appears to overlap between entries in the Union, State, or Concurrent lists.

questions.question: The Right to Information Act came into force in:
questions.explanation: The Right to Information Act, 2005 came into force on 12 October 2005. It gives citizens the right to access information held by public authorities.

questions.question: Which article enables the High Courts to issue writs?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers every High Court to issue writs including Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Certiorari, Prohibition, and Quo Warranto for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and for any other purpose.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India audits:
options: Accounts of the Union government, state governments, and government-owned companies
questions.explanation: The CAG (Article 148–151) audits accounts of Union, states, and all bodies substantially financed from public funds. The CAG report is presented to Parliament/state legislatures. The CAG is appointed by the President and removed by Parliament by the same process as SC judges.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established in:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established on 12 October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 (amended 2006). Its Chairperson is a retired Chief Justice of India. It investigates human rights violations, studies treaties, and recommends measures for protection.

questions.question: The 1st Constitutional Amendment (1951) was related to:
options: Adding Schedule 9 (to protect certain land reform laws from judicial review), restricting free speech on grounds of public order, and enabling reservations
questions.explanation: The 1st Amendment Act (1951) by Nehru: (1) Added 9th Schedule to protect land reform laws from court challenge, (2) added 'public order' and 'incitement to offence' as grounds to restrict free speech, (3) enabled state reservations for backward classes in educational institutions.

questions.question: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) is significant because:
options: It reversed several provisions of the 42nd Amendment, removed Right to Property from Fundamental Rights, strengthened Article 20-21 against emergency suspension
questions.explanation: 44th Amendment (1978, after Emergency): removed the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights (made legal right under Article 300A), changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion' in Art 352, restored rights under Art 19, 20, 21 against emergency suspension, gave press freedom during Emergency.

questions.question: The Anti-Defection Law is in:
questions.explanation: 10th Schedule (Anti-Defection Law) was added by 52nd Constitutional Amendment 1985 to prevent political defections. Members are disqualified for voluntarily giving up party membership or voting against party whip.

questions.question: Article 143 empowers the President to seek advisory opinion from:
questions.explanation: Article 143: The President can refer any question of law or fact of public importance to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion. The SC may give or refuse to give the opinion.

questions.question: Writ of 'Certiorari' is issued to:
options: Quash/review orders of lower courts or tribunals
Command a person to perform duty
questions.explanation: Certiorari (meaning 'to be certified'): issued by higher court to lower court/tribunal to quash its order on grounds of lack of jurisdiction, error of law, or violation of natural justice.

questions.question: 'Mandamus' writ is issued to:
options: Command a public authority to perform its duty
Transfer a case to another court
questions.explanation: Mandamus (Latin: 'we command') is issued to compel a public authority, inferior court, corporation, or government officer to perform a mandatory duty that they have failed to perform.

questions.question: The writ of 'Quo Warranto' is related to:
options: Challenging a person's legal right to hold a public office
questions.explanation: Quo Warranto (Latin: 'by what authority') questions the legal right of a person to hold a public office. It prevents illegal usurpation of public offices.

questions.question: The Election Commission of India is established under:
questions.explanation: Article 324: The Election Commission is a constitutional body with superintendence, direction and control of preparation of electoral rolls and conduct of elections to Parliament, state legislatures, and Presidential/VP elections.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) is established under:
questions.explanation: Article 148: CAG is a constitutional authority — controls the finances of India. CAG audits the accounts of Union and States. Reports are laid before Parliament/state legislatures.

questions.question: The 86th Constitutional Amendment (2002) relates to:
options: Right to Free and Compulsory Education
questions.explanation: 86th Amendment (2002): Added Article 21A (Free and Compulsory Education for 6-14 years as Fundamental Right), amended Article 45 DPSP, and added 11th Fundamental Duty.

questions.question: The 101st Constitutional Amendment (2016) introduced:
questions.explanation: 101st Constitutional Amendment (September 2016) introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST) — a comprehensive indirect tax replacing multiple central and state taxes.

questions.question: The 97th Constitutional Amendment (2011) related to:
questions.explanation: 97th Constitutional Amendment (2011) gave constitutional status to cooperative societies — added Part IXB (Articles 243ZH to 243ZT) and made right to form cooperatives a fundamental right.

questions.question: India follows which type of citizenship?
options: Single citizenship (no dual citizenship for states)
questions.explanation: India provides for single citizenship — every citizen is a citizen of India only (unlike USA where citizens have both national and state citizenship). This promotes national unity.

questions.question: The Overseas Citizenship of India (OCI) scheme was introduced in:
questions.explanation: OCI scheme was introduced in 2005 through amendment to the Citizenship Act 1955. It allows PIOs (Persons of Indian Origin) to live and work in India indefinitely (not full citizenship).

questions.question: The Mandal Commission recommended OBC reservation of:
questions.explanation: Mandal Commission (1979-80) recommended 27% reservation for OBCs (Other Backward Classes) in central government jobs. Implemented in 1990 by VP Singh government — total reservation with SC/ST became 49.5%.

questions.question: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) established:
questions.explanation: Indra Sawhney case (Mandal Commission case, 1992): SC upheld OBC reservation but: (a) 50% ceiling on reservations, (b) Excluded creamy layer from OBC reservation, (c) No reservation in promotions.

questions.question: Which case upheld the power of Parliament to amend Fundamental Rights (later overruled)?
questions.explanation: Sankari Prasad case (1951) and Sajjan Singh case (1965): SC held Parliament has power to amend any provision of the Constitution including Fundamental Rights. This was overruled in Golaknath (1967).

questions.question: In which case did SC hold that Parliament CANNOT abridge Fundamental Rights (later overruled)?
questions.explanation: Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967): SC (11-judge bench) held that Fundamental Rights cannot be amended by Parliament. This was overruled by Kesavananda Bharati (1973).

questions.question: The Chief Election Commissioner of India can be removed by:
options: Same process as a Supreme Court judge
questions.explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner can be removed only through the same process as a Supreme Court judge — by an address of both Houses of Parliament passed by special majority (Article 324).

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Proportionality' in Indian constitutional law means:
options: State's action must be proportionate to the objective pursued
questions.explanation: Doctrine of Proportionality: When restricting Fundamental Rights, the means (restrictions) must be proportionate to the end (objective). Excessive restriction beyond what is necessary is unconstitutional.

questions.question: The 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) changed the term 'internal disturbance' in Art 352 to:
questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment (1978) replaced 'internal disturbance' with 'armed rebellion' to make proclamation of Emergency more difficult. It also required Cabinet's written advice and Lok Sabha approval by 2/3rd majority.

questions.question: Financial Emergency (Art 360) has been invoked in India:
questions.explanation: Financial Emergency (Art 360) has never been proclaimed in India. It can be proclaimed when the financial stability or credit of India or any State is threatened.

questions.question: Education was transferred from State List to Concurrent List by which Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976) transferred Education from the State List to the Concurrent List, enabling Central government to legislate on education (Entry 25 of Concurrent List).

questions.question: The Finance Commission under Art 243I is constituted:
questions.explanation: Art 243I requires each State to constitute a Finance Commission every 5 years to review the financial position of Panchayats and make recommendations on tax devolution and grants.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was set up under:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. Its Chairperson must be a retired Chief Justice of India. The current Act was amended in 2019.

questions.question: Which Amendment introduced 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS)?
questions.explanation: The 103rd Constitutional Amendment (2019) added Arts 15(6) and 16(6), providing 10% reservation in educational institutions and government jobs for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) of general category.

questions.question: The 8th Schedule of the Constitution currently recognizes how many languages?
questions.explanation: The 8th Schedule currently recognizes 22 languages. Originally 14 languages at Constitution's commencement; Sindhi (21st Amendment, 1967), Konkani, Manipuri, Nepali (71st Amendment, 1992), Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, Santhali (92nd Amendment, 2003) were added.

questions.question: Which Amendment granted Constitutional status to Cooperative Societies?
questions.explanation: The 97th Amendment (2011) inserted Part IX-B (Arts 243ZH to 243ZT) dealing with Cooperative Societies and added 'right to form cooperative societies' in Art 19(1)(c) as a Fundamental Right.

questions.question: Which Article defines 'State' for the purpose of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 12 defines 'State' for Part III (Fundamental Rights) — it includes Government and Parliament of India, Government and Legislature of each State, and all local or other authorities within India or under the Government of India.

questions.question: Article 13 declares laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights to be:
options: Void to the extent of inconsistency
questions.explanation: Article 13 declares all laws (pre-Constitution laws and future laws) to be void to the extent they are inconsistent with Fundamental Rights. This is the basis for Judicial Review of legislation in India.

questions.question: Which writ literally means 'by what authority' and is used to challenge an appointment to a public office?
questions.explanation: Quo Warranto (meaning 'by what authority') is issued to inquire into the legality of claim of a person to a public office.

questions.question: Under the 'Emergency Provisions', how many National Emergencies have been declared in India?
questions.explanation: Three National Emergencies have been declared: 1962 (Chinese aggression), 1971 (Pakistani aggression), and 1975-1977 (internal disturbance — the only 'internal' Emergency).

questions.question: What is a 'Writ' in legal terms?
options: A court order requiring someone to do or stop doing something
questions.explanation: A writ is a formal written order issued by a court directing someone to perform or refrain from performing a specific act.

questions.question: Which symbol appears on the national emblem of India?
options: Ashoka's Lion Capital with the motto 'Satyameva Jayate'
questions.explanation: India's national emblem is adapted from the Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath. It shows four lions and the motto 'Satyameva Jayate' (Truth Alone Triumphs).

questions.question: Tamil is classified as a 'Classical Language' since:
questions.explanation: The Government of India recognized Tamil as the first Classical Language of India in 2004. Subsequently, Sanskrit, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, and Odia also received classical language status.

questions.question: Which states in India have a bicameral legislature (two houses)?
options: UP, Maharashtra, Bihar, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, J&K
questions.explanation: States with Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad): UP, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Bihar. J&K had bicameral legislature before Art 370 abrogation.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established by:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established by the Protection of Human Rights Act 1993. The Chairman of NHRC is a retired Chief Justice of India. It investigates human rights violations.

questions.question: Which writ is used to transfer a case from lower court to High Court?
questions.explanation: Certiorari can be used to transfer a case from a lower court to a superior court and to quash the order of the lower court if it has exceeded its jurisdiction or violated natural justice.

questions.question: The Sarkaria Commission (1983) was set up to examine:
questions.explanation: Sarkaria Commission (1983-87) was set up to examine and review Centre-State relations. It submitted its report in 1988 with 247 recommendations to strengthen cooperative federalism.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the Right to Equality?
questions.explanation: Article 14 guarantees equality before law and equal protection of laws.

questions.question: The right to freedom of speech and expression is subject to reasonable restrictions under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 19(2) lists the grounds (e.g., security of state, public order) on which restrictions can be imposed.

questions.question: Which fundamental right is suspended during a national emergency under Article 359?
questions.explanation: Article 359 allows suspension of enforcement of fundamental rights except Articles 20 and 21.

questions.question: The right to education as a fundamental right was added by which amendment?
questions.explanation: 86th Amendment inserted Article 21A, making education a fundamental right for children aged 6-14.

questions.question: Which article prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth?
questions.explanation: Article 15 prohibits discrimination by the State on these grounds.

questions.question: The Right to Freedom of Religion is guaranteed under which articles?
questions.explanation: Articles 25 to 28 deal with freedom of conscience, practice, and propagation of religion.

questions.question: Under Article 32, the Supreme Court can issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights. Which writ is issued to prevent a lower court from exceeding its jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Prohibition is a writ issued by a higher court to prevent a subordinate court from acting beyond its jurisdiction.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a fundamental right?
questions.explanation: Right to Property was removed as a fundamental right by the 44th Amendment and is now a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: The Right against Exploitation includes which of the following?
questions.explanation: Articles 23 and 24 prohibit human trafficking, forced labour, and child labour in hazardous industries.

questions.question: Which article allows the state to make special provisions for women and children?
questions.explanation: Article 15(3) empowers the State to make special provisions for women and children.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available only to citizens of India and not to foreign nationals?
options: Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression
Right to Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
questions.explanation: Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19 is available only to citizens, while Articles 14, 21, and 25 apply to all persons.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution of India is the Right to Constitutional Remedies guaranteed?
questions.explanation: Article 32 empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights, making it the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution.

questions.question: The Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution are borrowed from which country's constitution?
questions.explanation: The concept of Fundamental Rights is derived from the US Constitution, which also has a Bill of Rights.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a Fundamental Right under the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to Property was originally a Fundamental Right but was removed by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978 and is now a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: Which writ is issued by a court to command a public authority to perform a duty it is legally obligated to perform?
questions.explanation: Mandamus is a writ that orders a public official or body to carry out a mandatory duty, ensuring enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Indian Constitution is the Right to Education guaranteed as a Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Amendment Act, 2002, provides free and compulsory education to children aged 6-14 years.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a ground for reasonable restriction of the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19(2)?
questions.explanation: Right to property was removed as a ground for restriction by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978, and is no longer a Fundamental Right.

questions.question: The Right to Constitutional Remedies under Article 32 can be suspended during which of the following?
questions.explanation: Article 359 allows suspension of enforcement of Fundamental Rights (except Articles 20 and 21) during a National Emergency.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is specifically available to both citizens and non-citizens?
questions.explanation: Right against exploitation (Articles 23-24) prohibits trafficking and forced labour, applying to all persons, not just citizens.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution was propounded by the Supreme Court in which landmark case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution abolishes 'untouchability' and forbids its practice in any form?
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and makes its practice a punishable offence under the Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955.

questions.question: The Right to Freedom of Religion under Article 25 is subject to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Article 25 guarantees freedom of conscience and free profession, practice, and propagation of religion, subject to public order, morality, and health.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Directive Principle of State Policy and NOT a Fundamental Right?
options: Right to equal pay for equal work
questions.explanation: Right to equal pay for equal work is a Directive Principle under Article 39(d), not a Fundamental Right, though it is enforceable through judicial interpretation.

questions.question: Under the 73rd Amendment Act, 1992, which level of Panchayati Raj is mandatory in all states with a population above 20 lakh?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment mandates a three-tier system (Gram, Block, District) in states with a population above 20 lakh, as per Article 243B.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available only to citizens of India and not to foreigners?
options: Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression
Right to Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
questions.explanation: Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19 is available only to citizens; others like Articles 14, 21, and 25 are available to all persons.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution is the Right to Constitutional Remedies guaranteed?
questions.explanation: Article 32 confers the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights; Article 226 is for High Courts.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) was inserted by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment (2002) made education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6-14 years.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a ground for reasonable restriction on the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19(2)?
questions.explanation: Article 19(2) lists grounds like security of State, public order, defamation, contempt of court, etc.; 'right to privacy' is not a restriction ground.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'basic structure' of the Constitution, which limits Parliament's power to amend Fundamental Rights, was propounded in which case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended, including Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right prohibits trafficking in human beings and forced labour?
questions.explanation: Article 23 under Right against Exploitation prohibits trafficking, begar, and forced labour.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution are the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) contained?
questions.explanation: Directive Principles are in Part IV (Articles 36-51); Fundamental Rights are in Part III.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights can be suspended during a national emergency (except Articles 20 and 21)?
questions.explanation: Under Article 359, the President can suspend the enforcement of all Fundamental Rights except Articles 20 and 21 during a national emergency.

questions.question: The right to freedom of religion under Article 25 includes the right to propagate religion. Which of the following is a valid restriction?
questions.explanation: Article 25 allows restrictions based on public order, morality, and health, as well as other provisions of Part III.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Fundamental Duty under Article 51A, added by the 42nd Amendment?
questions.explanation: Article 51A lists duties including to safeguard public property and abjure violence; voting and taxes are not Fundamental Duties.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Indian Constitution can the President suspend the enforcement of Fundamental Rights during a national emergency?
questions.explanation: Article 359 authorizes the President to suspend the enforcement of Fundamental Rights (except Articles 20 and 21) during a national emergency proclaimed under Article 352.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a ground for imposing reasonable restrictions on the Right to Freedom of Religion (Article 25)?
questions.explanation: Article 25 allows restrictions on religious freedom only for grounds of public order, morality, and health, not for economic development.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) guarantees free and compulsory education to children in which age group?
questions.explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Amendment Act, 2002, guarantees free and compulsory education to all children aged 6 to 14 years.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to quash an order passed by a lower court or tribunal on grounds of lack of jurisdiction or error of law?
questions.explanation: Certiorari is a writ issued by a higher court to quash an order of a lower court or tribunal on grounds of jurisdictional error or illegality.

questions.question: In which landmark case did the Supreme of India establish the 'basic structure' doctrine, limiting Parliament's power to amend Fundamental Rights?
options: Shankari Prasad vs Union of India
S.R. Bommai vs Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala
Minerva Mills vs Union of India
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which of the following Directive Principles (DPSPs) in the Constitution of India is based on the principles of Gandhian socialism?
options: Equal pay for equal work for men and women
questions.explanation: Article 40, which directs the state to organize village panchayats and endow them with self-government, is rooted in Gandhian ideology.

questions.question: Under the 73rd Amendment Act, 1992, which authority is responsible for conducting elections to Panchayati Raj institutions in Tamil Nadu?
questions.explanation: The State Election Commission, established under Article 243K, is tasked with superintendence, direction, and control of panchayat elections in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right is violated if a person is arrested without being informed of the grounds of arrest, as per Article 22?
options: Right against arbitrary arrest and detention
questions.explanation: Article 22 guarantees protection against arbitrary arrest and detention, including the right to be informed of grounds of arrest and to consult a lawyer.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to Equality?
questions.explanation: Article 14 ensures equality before law and equal protection of laws.

questions.question: Under which Article is the practice of 'untouchability' abolished?
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a Fundamental Right in the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to Property was removed as a Fundamental Right by the 44th Amendment and is now a legal right.

questions.question: The Supreme Court declared the Right to Privacy as part of which Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: In K.S. Puttaswamy case, the Supreme Court held that the Right to Privacy is intrinsic to Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).

questions.question: Which writ is issued to quash an order of a lower court or tribunal?
questions.explanation: Certiorari is issued to set aside or quash the order of a subordinate court or tribunal.

questions.question: Who described the Right to Constitutional Remedies (Article 32) as the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called Article 32 the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution.

questions.question: Under which Article can a person directly approach a High Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: The Right to Freedom of Religion under Article 25 is subject to which set of limitations?
questions.explanation: Article 25 provides freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion, subject to public order, morality and health.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution of India guarantees the Right to Equality before the law?
questions.explanation: Article 14 ensures equality before law and equal protection of laws. Article 15 prohibits discrimination, Article 16 equality of opportunity, Article 17 abolishes untouchability.

questions.question: The 'Right to Education' as a Fundamental Right under Article 21A was inserted by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment Act, 2002 inserted Article 21A providing free and compulsory education to children aged 6-14 years.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution can the Parliament impose reasonable restrictions on Fundamental Rights in the interest of public order?
questions.explanation: Article 19(2) allows restrictions on freedom of speech and expression for reasons including public order. Others relate to assembly, association, and movement respectively.

questions.question: The landmark Supreme Court case 'Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala' (1973) established which doctrine regarding Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati case held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended, including certain Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a Fundamental Right under Part III of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to Property was removed as a fundamental right by the 44th Amendment (1978) and made a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Laches' in the context of Fundamental Rights means?
options: Delay in filing a petition may bar relief
Only citizens can file a writ
Fundamental Rights are suspended during Emergency
No restriction on approaching Supreme Court
questions.explanation: The doctrine of laches implies that unreasonable delay in approaching the court may lead to denial of relief.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution provides for the 'Right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights'?
questions.explanation: Article 32 guarantees the right to approach the Supreme Court directly for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Article 226 gives similar power to High Courts.

questions.question: The 'Right to Privacy' has been declared as a Fundamental Right under which Article by the Supreme Court in the Justice K.S. Puttaswamy case?
questions.explanation: In 2017, the Supreme Court held that right to privacy is an intrinsic part of Article 21 (Right to life and personal liberty).

questions.question: In the context of Fundamental Rights, the term 'State' under Article 12 does NOT include?
questions.explanation: Article 12 defines 'State' to include Government, Parliament, State Legislatures, and local authorities, but not private individuals.

questions.question: The right to constitutional remedies is considered the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution by which jurist?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called Article 32 the heart and soul of the Constitution.

questions.question: Under which Article is the Right to Freedom of Religion guaranteed?
questions.explanation: Article 25 provides freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available only to citizens and not to foreigners?
questions.explanation: Article 19 freedoms (speech, assembly, etc.) are available only to citizens.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a Fundamental Right under Part III of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to property was removed from fundamental rights by the 44th Amendment and is now a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.question: The writ of 'Habeas Corpus' is issued to:
options: produce a detained person before court
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus means 'to have the body' and is used to release a person from illegal detention.

questions.question: Which Article prohibits trafficking in human beings and forced labour?
questions.explanation: Article 23 prohibits traffic in human beings and begar (forced labour).

questions.question: The Fundamental Rights can be suspended during a national emergency except which two Articles?
questions.explanation: During emergency, Articles 20 (protection in conviction) and 21 (right to life) cannot be suspended.

questions.question: Which case established that Fundamental Rights cannot be amended by Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) introduced the basic structure doctrine, limiting Parliament's power to amend fundamental rights.

questions.question: The 'Right to Education' as a Fundamental Right under Article 21A applies to children of which age group?
questions.explanation: Article 21A guarantees free and compulsory education to all children aged 6 to 14 years, added by the 86th Amendment Act, 2002.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued by a court to quash the order of a lower court or tribunal on grounds of lack of jurisdiction or error of law?
questions.explanation: Certiorari is a writ issued to quash the order of a lower court or tribunal when it has acted beyond its jurisdiction or committed an error of law.

questions.question: The Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution can be suspended during a national emergency, except which of the following?
options: Right to Life and Personal Liberty (Article 21)
questions.explanation: Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) cannot be suspended even during a national emergency, as per the 44th Amendment Act, 1978.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a ground for imposing reasonable restrictions on the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19(2)?
questions.explanation: Article 19(2) lists grounds such as sovereignty, security, public order, decency, morality, contempt of court, defamation, and incitement to an offence; 'right to privacy' is not a listed ground.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Eclipse' under Article 13 applies to which of the following?
options: Pre-constitutional laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
Post-constitutional laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
Customary laws inconsistent with Directive Principles
Judicial decisions inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Eclipse states that pre-constitutional laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights are not void ab initio but are eclipsed and become dormant until the inconsistency is removed.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is specifically available to minorities in India?
options: Right to establish and administer educational institutions (Article 30)
Right to move freely throughout India (Article 19)
questions.explanation: Article 30 grants linguistic and religious minorities the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.

questions.question: In the context of Fundamental Rights, the term 'State' under Article 12 includes all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Private companies registered under Companies Act
questions.explanation: Article 12 defines 'State' to include Government, Parliament, State Legislatures, and local authorities, but not private companies unless they perform public functions.

questions.question: Which of the following amendments to the Constitution of India added the word 'secular' to the Preamble?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act, 1976 added the words 'socialist', 'secular', and 'integrity' to the Preamble of the Constitution.

questions.question: The Right to Education (Article 21A) was inserted by which Constitutional Amendment Act?
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment Act, 2002 inserted Article 21A, making free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14 years.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued by a court to a public authority to perform a public duty that it has failed to perform?
questions.explanation: Mandamus is a writ commanding a public authority to perform its statutory duty, while Habeas Corpus deals with illegal detention, Prohibition prevents lower courts from exceeding jurisdiction, and Certiorari quashes orders of lower courts.

questions.question: The Fundamental Rights under the Indian Constitution can be suspended during a national emergency, except which of the following?
questions.explanation: During a national emergency under Article 359, the President can suspend enforcement of Fundamental Rights, but Articles 20 (protection against conviction) and 21 (right to life) cannot be suspended.

questions.question: The Right against Exploitation under Articles 23 and 24 prohibits which of the following?
options: Traffic in human beings and forced labour
Child labour below 14 years in factories
questions.explanation: Article 23 prohibits traffic in human beings and forced labour, while Article 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or hazardous occupations.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Right to Freedom of Religion (Articles 25-28) is correct?
options: It allows the state to interfere in religious matters for social reform
It guarantees absolute freedom to practice any religion without any restriction
It prohibits the state from making any law relating to religious institutions
It allows religious instruction in state-funded educational institutions
questions.explanation: Article 25 permits the state to regulate secular activities associated with religious practice and to provide for social welfare and reform, such as abolishing untouchability.

questions.question: The concept of 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution, which limits Parliament's power to amend Fundamental Rights, was established in which landmark case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)
Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994)
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights, though it can amend individual provisions.

questions.question: Under the Tamil Nadu Panchayati Raj Act, which tier of the three-tier system is mandatory for all states?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment mandates the three-tier system, but the intermediate tier (Panchayat Samiti) is not compulsory for states with a population below 20 lakhs; the village panchayat is the basic mandatory unit.

questions.question: Under which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India are the Fundamental Rights guaranteed?
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights are enshrined in Part III of the Constitution, covering Articles 12 to 35.

questions.question: The right to move the Supreme Court for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights is guaranteed under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 32 confers the right to approach the Supreme Court directly for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights under the Indian Constitution is available only to citizens of India and not to foreign nationals?
options: Right to freedom of speech and expression
Right to life and personal liberty
Right to equality before the law
questions.explanation: Article 19 (right to freedom of speech, etc.) is available only to citizens, whereas Articles 14, 21, and 25 extend to all persons.

questions.question: In which case did the Supreme Court hold that the Fundamental Rights cannot be amended even by a constitutional amendment under Article 368?
questions.explanation: In Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967), the Supreme Court ruled that Fundamental Rights are transcendental and immutable, thus beyond amending power.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution is the Right to Education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14 years?
questions.explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Amendment Act, 2002, makes education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6-14 years.

questions.question: Which of the following Directive Principles is based on the principle of 'equal pay for equal work'?
questions.explanation: Article 39(d) directs the State to ensure equal pay for equal work for both men and women.

questions.question: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, added which Part to the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment inserted Part IX (Articles 243 to 243-O) dealing with Panchayats.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a ground for reasonable restriction of the Right to Freedom of Speech under Article 19(2)?
questions.explanation: Article 19(2) allows restrictions on grounds of sovereignty, security, public order, decency, morality, contempt of court, defamation, and incitement to an offence—not privacy.

questions.question: In the context of Tamil Nadu, which act governs the conduct of elections to local bodies?
questions.explanation: The Tamil Nadu Panchayats Act, 1994, along with the Tamil Nadu Municipal Laws, governs local body elections in the state.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Preamble of the Indian Constitution is correct?
options: It was adopted on 26 November 1949 and amended in 1976
It is enforceable in a court of law
It declares India a socialist, secular, democratic republic
questions.explanation: The Preamble was adopted on 26 Nov 1949, and the words 'socialist' and 'secular' were added by the 42nd Amendment in 1976. It is not enforceable in court.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, the power to remove a Supreme Court judge lies with:
options: The President on advice of the Chief Justice
The President after impeachment by both Houses of Parliament
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge can be removed only by the President after an address by each House of Parliament supported by a special majority on grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity.

questions.question: Which of the following Tamil Nadu government policies is directly linked to the Directive Principles of State Policy?
options: Tamil Nadu Free Breakfast Scheme for school children
questions.explanation: The Free Breakfast Scheme aligns with Article 39(e) and (f) and Article 47, which direct the State to secure the health and nutrition of children.

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available only to citizens and not to foreigners, and has been held by the Supreme Court to be non-existent in the case of State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar (1952) in the context of the right to equality before law?
options: Right to equality of opportunity in public employment under Article 16
Right to freedom of speech under Article 19
Right to protection of life and personal liberty under Article 21
questions.explanation: Article 16 (equality of opportunity in public employment) is available only to citizens. In Anwar Ali Sarkar case, the Court upheld classification under Article 14 but struck down discriminatory procedure, not Article 16.

questions.question: In the context of the fundamental right to freedom of religion under Article 25, which of the following correctly represents the scope of 'essential religious practices' as interpreted by the Supreme Court in the Shirur Mutt case (1954)?
options: All practices mandated by religious scripture are protected
Only practices integral to the religion, determined by the court, are protected
Practices declared essential by the religious denomination are final
Only practices existing at the time of the Constitution's commencement are protected
questions.explanation: The Shirur Mutt case held that the court decides whether a practice is essential to religion, not the religious denomination, and only those integral practices are protected under Article 25.

questions.question: Under Article 31C, as inserted by the 25th Amendment and upheld in Kesavananda Bharati (1973), which of the following statements is correct regarding the status of Directive Principles vis-à-vis Fundamental Rights?
options: All Directive Principles prevail over all Fundamental Rights
Only Articles 39(b) and 39(c) prevail over Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19, and 31, subject to judicial review
Directive Principles completely override Fundamental Rights without any judicial review
Article 31C was struck down in its entirety by the Supreme Court
questions.explanation: Article 31C protects laws giving effect to Articles 39(b) and (c) from being challenged under Articles 14, 19, and 31, but such laws are subject to judicial review on the ground of 'manifest arbitrariness' per later rulings.

questions.question: In the context of the 73rd Constitutional Amendment (Panchayati Raj), which of the following provisions is NOT a fundamental right but a constitutional directive, yet the Supreme Court in State of U.P. v. Pradhan Sangh Kshetra Samiti (1995) held it to be mandatory for the state to implement?
options: Reservation of seats for SC/ST in Panchayats
Establishment of a State Finance Commission every five years
Gram Sabha as a body corporate
questions.explanation: Gram Sabha as a body corporate is a constitutional mandate under Article 243A, not a fundamental right, but its mandatory nature was upheld by the Supreme Court for grassroots democracy.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the Preamble and Fundamental Rights as interpreted by the Supreme Court in the Berubari Union case (1960) and later in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973)?
options: Preamble is a part of the Constitution and can be enforced as a Fundamental Right
Preamble is not a source of power but can be used to clarify ambiguities in Fundamental Rights
